name: Security Misconfiguration
what_it_means: Unsafe defaults or forgotten hardening (open CORS, verbose errors, unused methods, missing headers, hardcoded secrets).
animated_example: Leaving windows open after installing a new security door.
quick_checks: Disable unused HTTP methods (TRACE, PUT/DELETE if unused)
Secrets managed in vault; not in code/images/env files
how_to_test: Preflight CORS probes; attempt cross-origin reads
Trigger errors and inspect for stack traces/paths
Probe OPTIONS/TRACE; scan for header gaps and configs
expected_outcomes: Minimal information exposure; tight method/origin controls
No secrets or environment details leaked
minimal_example: Wildcard CORS + verbose SQL error → misconfiguration; fix to strict origin + generic error
mitigation: Lock down CORS policy to an explicit allowlist of trusted origins with defined methods and headers — never use wildcard (*) in production or for credentialed requests

Apply the full security response header set (HSTS with preload, Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Referrer-Policy) and validate headers automatically post-deploy with a scanning tool

Manage all secrets (API keys, DB credentials, certificates) in a dedicated secrets vault (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) — never commit secrets to source code, container images, or CI environment variables

Implement configuration-as-code with security linting gates in the CI/CD pipeline to catch insecure defaults, exposed debug endpoints, or missing hardening settings before any deployment reaches production
